pullRequest.title: [FEATURE] FFMPEG Transcoding Support
pullRequest.body:
# Description
This request is to add support for using FFmpeg for transcoding as an alternative to Handbrake
This enables user to choose an option different to the Handbrake default and holds a number of other advantages including

- Better community support for headless and automated applications
- Better support for hardware acceleration including Intel quick-sync and AMD AMF which seem to have quite temperamental support on handbrake especially for newer hardware

Fixes #945 
Fixes #1406 

## Type of change

- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [x] This change requires a documentation update

# How Has This Been Tested?

I have tested this on docker using docker-compose with a custom dockerfile to enable Intel Hardware acceleration, but due to the nature the docker deployment and assuming correct drivers are installed for hardware acceleration no other problems should arise for other hardware acceleration types NVENC, AMF, maybe even Arm64 Acceleration at later points but custom arguments and default may need to be selected

pullRequest.title: [BUGFIX] Fix MINLENGTH for track.process
pullRequest.body:
# Description
Commit d087730 removed MINLENGTH from being passed to makemkv for single track.
As a result, makemkv would use default of 120s for such conditions.
If the user specifies MINLENGTH <120s, unexpected behavior occurs, such as jobs erroring out and jobs finishing without the desired titles.

I don't know if there is any particular reason for the original removal, so please confirm whether there is some case I haven't considered. But it resolved the issue in my testing.

Fixes #1299

pullRequest.title: [FEATURE] Drive Identification and Refactoring
pullRequest.body:
# Description
- USB devices frequently change their mount points on system reboot, causing faulty job associations to drives. To prevent this, drive recognition based on pyudev serial information (maker, model, serial number) of optical drives is introduced.
- MakeMKV info calls create zombie processes and syslog errors at MakeMKV level. These changes help to reduce the amount of MakeMKV info calls getting in each other's way.

## Type of change

- [X] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [X] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [X] This change requires a documentation update

# How Has This Been Tested?

- [X ] Docker

# Checklist:

- [X] My code follows the style guidelines of this project
- [X] I have performed a self-review of my own code
- [X] I have commented my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas
- [X] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation
- [X] My changes generate no new warnings
- [X] I have tested that my fix is effective or that my feature works

# Changelog:

- Handle race condition of concurrent MakeMKV info calls creating zombie processes and silently failing.
- Enhance MakeMKV Calls with Parser and Error Handling.
- Reduce MakeMKV info calls by caching the MakeMKV drive id on calls to disc:9999
- Handle alternating mount points for USB devices.
- Add support for displaying more device info
- On system startup, scan drives to recognize hardware changes. Mark unfound devices as stale and delete their last mount point.
- The layout of the drive tab was changed to include more information on the drives.
- DataClasses and Enums are used for better usability of the MakeMKV and pyudev output.

# Changelog:

This wraps the psutil functions into its own try-catch loop. After the
psutil loop, a process is either terminated or an error occurred that is
not yet handled.
If the process is terminated, we return "true". If an error occurred, we
revert the database so that the job will still be visible and the user
can try to kill it once more.

The prerequisite for releasing the drive from the current job is that the
associated process is terminated. Otherwise the drive is not released.
Releasing the drive from the job is completely handled by job.eject().
No call to a (possibly non-existing) associated drive is done on the
abandon-layer.

# Description
Adds new tools for finding filenames with minor naming discrepencies that can occur where Handbrake's output filenames may differ from what is stored in the database or derived by the system

Fixes #1530 

## Type of change
- [X] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)

# How Has This Been Tested?
Built image locally and tested with discs known to trigger error #1530 as well as discs that processed normally before any changes. Successfully finds filenames that previously caused jobs to fail on final file copy (but showed as success).
- [X] Docker
- [ ] Other (Please state here)

# Checklist:
- [X] My code follows the style guidelines of this project
- [X] I have performed a self-review of my own code
- [X] I have commented my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas
- [X] I have made corresponding changes to the documentation
- [X] My changes generate no new warnings
- [X] I have tested that my fix is effective or that my feature works

# Changelog:
Fixed
- Track filename mismatch: When the actual file name differs slightly (e.g. by a few characters) from the expected name, ARM now attempts a fuzzy-matching fallback instead of failing outright.
- File-moving logic: move_files now uses find_matching_file to locate and move “near-match” filenames.
- HandBrake integration: Minor adjustments to handbrake-related routines to better align with the file-matching fallback.

Changes & Improvements
- Added _calculate_filename_similarity helper to compute a matching score between basename strings.
- Introduced find_matching_file function that scans candidate files in the target directory with the same extension and selects the best match if similarity is high enough.
- Updated tests in test_ripper_utils_file_matching.py to cover edge cases around mismatch detection.

Version bump: 2.20.4 → 2.20.5

- This is an example of a disc where a filename mismatch did not occur, and the system proceeds normally:
[JASON_ARGONAUTS.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22728134/JASON_ARGONAUTS.log)

pullRequest.title: [BUGFIX] CPU info not displayed when using rpi4
pullRequest.body:
# Description
Currently, CPU model won't be found when run on some Raspberrypis, the output of `cat /proc/cpuinfo` is slightly different under Rpi
I added a check for `Model` in the regex, It's after the initial check for `model name` so this shouldn't change anything for other installations. Tested/updated system info, and it now works correctly with the changes.

Fixes #1597

pullRequest.title: [BUGFIX] resolve x86 CPU Model Name Bug
pullRequest.body:
`re.IGNORECASE` causes a hit on the CPU model number before the CPU name (this has been edited per my most recent comment on this PR). On a 6th-gen x86 Intel CPU, the CPU ID is 94, not a CPU model string.

# Description
I would like to update `arm/models/system_info.py` to fix a regex issue when ARM is running in Docker on x86 CPUs. Currently, the model _number_ is displayed instead of the CPU name (not expected behavior). 

Context: I was wondering why the CPU was listed as 94 instead of as a 6th gen i5. 
Motivation: I realized it was a bug.

Fixes #1646 (admittedly, I also created this issue a bit ago.)

## Type of change
- [ x ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)

# How Has This Been Tested?
I used a python shell with the relevant cpuinfo data dumped to a text file as well as a few different regex calculators online. As this is a relatively minor change, I didn't redeploy arm to validate. I am using Docker on Debian Trixie.
